title: The Idle Hands
description: Automation has outrun the population. A generation has grown up with too little to do and too much time to wonder why they are here.

outcomes.label: Fund make-work projects
outcomes.description: Spend credits on purpose, even invented purpose.
outcomes.log: The council funded a hundred small projects. Idle hands found rails to paint and songs to write.

outcomes.label: Let them find their own purpose
outcomes.description: Trust boredom to breed either art or trouble.
outcomes.log: Left to themselves, the idle generation invented new things to be, some of them useful.

title: Too Many Hands
description: This cohort trained hard for posts that are already filled. The waiting list for real work is longer than a career.

outcomes.label: Rotate the posts
outcomes.description: Share the work thin so everyone gets some.
outcomes.log: The posts were rotated so the trained could stay sharp. Nobody is idle; nobody is expert.

title: The Lobites
description: A hold-stowed pair of lobites — soft, round, endlessly agreeable, and endlessly breeding — has become a hold-filling thousand. They eat little and purr, but there are so very many.

outcomes.label: Cull the warren
outcomes.description: Thin them back to a manageable few.
outcomes.log: The lobites were culled to a sensible number. The children cried; the quartermaster did not.

outcomes.label: Keep them all
outcomes.description: Morale is worth a little spilled grain.
outcomes.log: The ship kept its lobites. The corridors are louder, warmer, and slightly stickier for it.

title: The Inspection
description: A charter clerk, aboard since departure and forgotten by everyone, has emerged to conduct the mandated mid-voyage audit. He has a clipboard. He is very serious.

outcomes.label: Humor the audit
outcomes.description: Give him his forms and his afternoon.
outcomes.log: The clerk filed a forty-page report no one will read. He seemed, at last, content.

outcomes.label: Lose the paperwork
outcomes.description: Regretfully, the archive ate his forms.
outcomes.log: The audit was tragically lost to a filing error. The ship ran on, unaudited and unbothered.

title: Lost in Translation
description: An automated relay greeting, kept running for centuries, has been quietly mistranslating the ship's name as something deeply unflattering to everyone who hears it.

outcomes.label: Rewrite the greeting
outcomes.description: Restore a little dignity.
outcomes.log: The greeting was corrected. Somewhere, a relay operator stopped laughing at last.

title: The Ship's Cat
description: A corridor animal — no one is certain what species — has adopted the bridge crew, the night shift, and, apparently, the entire ship. It answers to no name and every name.

outcomes.label: Make it official
outcomes.description: Enter it in the crew manifest.
outcomes.log: The animal was entered on the manifest with the rank of Morale Officer. It has not reported for duty once, and morale has never been higher.

title: The Understudy
description: The ship's ceremonial First Voice has died, and the only trained understudy is a nine-year-old who has memorized every word and refuses to be replaced.

outcomes.label: Let the child serve
outcomes.description: A child First Voice. The elders will cope.
outcomes.log: A nine-year-old read the founding words in a piping voice, and half the ship wept and the other half grinned. The role was never quite solemn again.

outcomes.label: Appoint a proper elder
outcomes.description: Dignity restored, the child heartbroken.
outcomes.log: A grave elder took the First Voice, and a small understudy was told to wait forty years for a turn.

title: The Long Table
description: The Hearth Union petitions to make the shared evening meal sacrosanct — every soul at the long table, every night, no shift or duty excepted. Community over output, the remembered name over the quota.

outcomes.label: Set the long table
outcomes.description: Down tools each evening. The ship eats as one.
outcomes.log: The long table was set every night, and duty bent around supper. The ship lost some hours of labor and gained a century of belonging; the names were remembered at the table.

outcomes.label: Keep the shifts running
outcomes.description: The work does not stop for supper. Eat in rotation.
outcomes.log: The council kept the shifts turning through the dinner hour. The quotas held and the Hearth Union set a smaller table, and set it a little colder.

title: The Great Bake-Off
description: A friendly rivalry between two galley decks over the ship's best ration-loaf has escalated into a full-blown culinary schism, complete with partisans, sabotage, and a contested trophy. The council is asked, with total seriousness, to adjudicate.

outcomes.label: Judge the contest
outcomes.description: Taste, deliberate, and crown a champion loaf.
outcomes.log: The council convened a solemn tasting and crowned a champion loaf to citywide acclaim and one deck's lasting grievance. Morale soared on a tide of bread.

outcomes.label: Declare it a tie
outcomes.description: Diplomatically insist both loaves are equally supreme.
outcomes.log: The council declared the bake-off a perfect tie, and both galley decks united in shared disappointment at the cowardice of it. Peace, of a grumbling sort, was kept.

title: The Festival War
description: Two decks' harvest festivals, drifted apart over generations, have landed in the same week, and each insists the other moved. The galley cannot cater both, and neither will yield the date.

outcomes.label: Let them compete
outcomes.description: Rival festivals, one week, may the best deck win.
outcomes.log: Both festivals ran, louder than ever out of pure spite, and the whole ship ate twice. Morale soared; the decks are not speaking, but they are not speaking very cheerfully.

outcomes.label: Fuse them into one
outcomes.description: One holiday, ship-wide, that nobody's grandparents ever kept.
outcomes.log: The council fused the two festivals into a single ship-wide holiday with a new name and older-sounding songs written last month. Within a generation no one will remember it was ever two — which is how all traditions start.

title: The Reconstructed Feast
description: The key spice for the founders' feast-day dish ran out lifetimes ago. A cook unveils a faithful reconstruction from ship stores. It tastes nothing like the archived recipe, and the mess deck has never been happier.

outcomes.label: Call it the real thing
outcomes.description: It is delicious. Let it be the feast.
outcomes.log: The reconstruction was declared the true feast-day dish by acclamation, the archive quietly overruled by the nose. The founders would not recognize a bite of it, and would, after one bite, forgive the ship entirely.

outcomes.label: Honor the archive
outcomes.description: Ration a substitute closer to what the founders ate.
outcomes.log: The archivists were consulted, the nearest substitute rationed out, and the feast served as the founders meant it — drier, greyer, correct. The old families were satisfied. Everyone else ate quietly and remembered last year's.

title: The Office of Lost Things
description: Without anyone ordering it, a generation has grown an elaborate bureau for cataloguing and returning lost tools and socks. It has forms now, a filing berth, and a proud staff of eleven.

outcomes.label: Make it real infrastructure
outcomes.description: Give the Office a budget and a mandate. Tools that stop vanishing are tools that stop being remade.
outcomes.log: The Office of Lost Things got a berth, a seal, and a line in the manifest. To general surprise it works — the shops stop bleeding tools, the fabricators run a shift lighter, and eleven people who were doing this anyway now do it on the record.

outcomes.label: Reassign the eleven
outcomes.description: Send the staff to real posts. The ship can find its own socks.
outcomes.log: The Office was thanked and disbanded, its eleven reassigned to posts that were genuinely short-handed. The filing berth is a store room again. People still turn up at the old hatch with lost spanners, and stand a moment, and go.

title: The False Noon
description: The navigators announce the ship has passed the exact mathematical midpoint of the crossing — a moment that changes nothing, that no one can see, in a voyage with no days. A committee of the bored has proposed making a holiday of it. The council must rule on the calendar.

outcomes.label: Declare the Great Midpoint
outcomes.description: A blowout for the halfway mark — the biggest feast the ship has thrown since launch nobody remembers.
outcomes.log: The Great Midpoint was declared, and the ship threw itself a party for arriving at a mathematical point in the void that looked exactly like every other point. It was glorious. Within two generations children will believe it was always a holiday, which is the truest kind of tradition.

outcomes.label: A working toast and back to shift
outcomes.description: Mark it in the log, raise one cup on the mess deck, and keep the watch. Halfway is halfway.
outcomes.log: The midpoint got a line in the log and a single raised cup on each mess deck, and then the watch went back on. It was the sort of restraint the founders would have approved of, and the sort the party faction has not entirely forgiven.

title: The Golden Watch
description: A census turns up a quiet marvel: a whole generation has come up knowing only good years — full larders, systems that hum, not one schism or starving winter in living memory. Someone proposes the ship name it what it plainly is, a golden age, and mark it before it passes, because good fortune noticed is good fortune half-kept.

outcomes.label: Name it, and make it a holiday
outcomes.description: Set aside a day, strike a plaque, teach the children that this was the ship at its best — a high-water mark to remember and to live up to. A people that knows it has been happy is a steadier people.
outcomes.log: The ship named its golden age while it was still living in it, and struck the day into the calendar — a plaque, a feast, a story the children would inherit: that once, for a while, everything worked, and everyone knew it. Years later, in leaner times, the memory of having been happy would steady hands that had never seen the plenty firsthand.

outcomes.label: Spend the good fortune on something great
outcomes.description: A golden age hoarded is only nostalgia in waiting. Pour the surplus and the high spirits into something the ship could never risk in a hard year — a grand refit, a bold survey, an ambition worthy of the good times.
outcomes.log: The ship spent its golden years rather than framing them — threw the surplus and the good will at an ambition it would never have dared in a lean season, and came out the other side changed and proud and a little poorer in ease. The plenty was gone, but the ship had made something of it, which is the other way to keep a good year.

title: The Flowering
description: In the fat years the ship's hands have found time for more than survival, and it shows: a deck-opera nobody sanctioned, three new festivals invented from whole cloth, a run of murals turning the maintenance corridors into something worth walking slowly through. The ship is, quietly and for the first time, making art. The question of whether to feed it comes to the council.

outcomes.label: Feed the flowering
outcomes.description: Give the deck-artists berths, time, and a little of the surplus, and let the ship become a place that makes things as well as one that survives. A culture worth carrying home is worth a few work-hours.
outcomes.log: The council fed the flowering — freed hands, found time, spent a little plenty on the making of beautiful useless things — and the ship became, over a generation, a place with a culture of its own: songs no founder wrote, holidays no charter named, a way of being that belonged to the voyage and to nowhere else. It would arrive home carrying more than cargo.

outcomes.label: A working celebration only
outcomes.description: Enjoy the good spirits, by all means — but the voyage is not a festival and the hands are needed. Let the arts be a evening's diversion, not a calling, and keep the ship pointed at its charter.
outcomes.log: The council let the good spirits run to an evening's diversion and no further, and kept the ship's hands to its charter through the fat years as through the lean. The deck-opera folded; the murals stayed, unfinished and fond. The voyage stayed exactly what the founders launched, which some counted a discipline and some a small closed door.

title: The Hearth's Feast
description: The Hearth Union has been glad aboard this ship, and gladness in that people takes one form above all others: they want to feed everyone. Their elders come to the council with a proposal to throw open the long tables — a feast for every deck, the grow-stores spent down for one great shared meal, the kind of festival the founders kept to mark a safe passage. It is a gift freely offered; the only question is how big to let it grow.

outcomes.label: Throw the doors wide
outcomes.description: Let the whole ship feast — spend the stores, and let the decks remember what plenty feels like.
outcomes.log: The Hearth opened every table and the ship came to eat. It cost the grow-stores a season's careful margin, and it was worth it — for a few days the corridors sounded like a people glad to be alive together, and half-remembering why.

outcomes.label: A modest observance
outcomes.description: Accept the gift, but keep it small — a shared meal, not a feast, the stores mostly held.
outcomes.log: The council thanked the Hearth and kept the feast modest — one good meal, the stores mostly held for leaner days. The gesture landed; the lift was gentler than the Hearth had hoped, but no one went without, and no one starved for it later.
